data.title: Japanese Yen Weakens Ahead of Key US Jobs Report
data.description:
The Japanese yen slipped toward 144 per dollar on Friday, extending losses from the previous session as traders adopted a cautious stance ahead of the closely watched US monthly jobs report.

Sentiment was also influenced by a phone call between US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, in which the two leaders agreed to continue trade negotiations, though no concrete details were provided.

On the domestic front, data showed that Japan’s household spending unexpectedly declined in April, as rising prices continued to weigh on consumer demand.

The soft reading adds to the list of factors the Bank of Japan is monitoring as it assesses the timing of its next interest rate hike.

Still, BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda reaffirmed earlier this week that the central bank stands ready to raise rates if economic and inflation forecasts are met, reinforcing expectations of a cautious but steady policy tightening path.

data.title: PTT Global Chemical Faces Prolonged Petrochemical Downcycle — Market Talk
data.description: PTT Global Chemical faces a prolonged petrochemical downcycle, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Chak Reungsinpinya says in a report. This downcycle may last until 2028 and possibly much longer, owing to massive capacity additions and limited capacity closures, the analyst says. Also, the petrochemical and oil refining company doesn't look very attractive, as its stock trades at 0.68x price-to-tangible book value, the second-most expensive among Thailand downstream players. The brokerage cuts its 2025 earnings estimate for the Thai company by 13% to reflect its lower gross refining margin forecast. It downgrades the stock's rating to sell from hold, with an unchanged target price of THB16.00. Shares last closed at THB20.20. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0254 GMT - PTT Global Chemical faces a prolonged petrochemical downcycle, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Chak Reungsinpinya says in a report. This downcycle may last until 2028 and possibly much longer, owing to massive capacity additions and limited capacity closures, the analyst says. Also, the petrochemical and oil refining company doesn't look very attractive, as its stock trades at 0.68x price-to-tangible book value, the second-most expensive among Thailand downstream players. The brokerage cuts its 2025 earnings estimate for the Thai company by 13% to reflect its lower gross refining margin forecast. It downgrades the stock's rating to sell from hold, with an unchanged target price of THB16.00. Shares last closed at THB20.20. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0238 GMT - Malaysia's stock market is poised for portfolio rebalancing, after FTSE Russell announced that AMMB will replace Hong Leong Financial Group in the benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index following its June semiannual review, say CIMB Securities analysts Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia in a note. The change, effective June 23, could trigger rebalancing activities by passive and index-tracking funds involving the affected constituents, they note. They expect AMMB to carry a higher KLCI weight of 2.7%, compared with Hong Leong's 0.7%. Historical trends suggest that newly added KLCI stocks often outperform. CIMB maintains its end-2025 KLCI target of 1560 and favors CelcomDigi, Public Bank and Tenaga Nasional. The KLCI is 0.1% lower at 1516.27. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0216 GMT - Marco Polo Marine's FY2025 deployment of a new commissioning service operation vessel appears positive, RHB Research's Alfie Yeo says in a report. The vessel has secured charters at robust rates for the next three years and is taking more orders beyond that. The marine logistics group's earnings are likely to be supported by ship chartering from the rest of its fleet, driven by better utilization and charter rates, more vessels, and higher drydock capacity. RHB maintains a buy rating on the stock while trimming the target price to S$0.07 from S$0.08 to reflect reduced FY 2025-2027 earnings forecasts stemming from weaker-than-expected 1H FY 2025 results. Shares are unchanged at S$0.044. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0122 GMT - DFI Retail Group may benefit from the sale of its minority stake in Robinsons Retail in the Philippines, UOB Kay Hian's Adrian Loh says. The sale should enable the Asian retailer to continue its strategic refocus on higher-return core businesses, the analyst says. Despite selling the stake at a discount to the entry cost, the transaction underpins the Singapore-listed company's shift toward return on capital employed-accretive operations. With 2025 profit growth guided at 14%-34% and net debt now eliminated, DFI Retail Group looks well-placed for growth, improved margins and dividend upside. The brokerage raises the stock's target price to US$3.50 from US$2.80 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are unchanged at US$2.66. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0034 GMT - Dundee Precious Metals may not be the only party interested in Adriatic Metals, says Canaccord Genuity. Adriatic recently confirmed it's in talks with Dundee about a potential takeover bid. That led to Adriatic's share price rising some 40%. Canaccord says Dundee has always looked like a potential bidder for Adriatic given similarities between their European assets. "However, we suspect other parties may also be interested in Adriatic's high-grade, low-cost operation," analyst Paul Howard says. Canaccord lifts its price target for Adriatic by 21% to A$5.50/share. That values its equity at US$1.25 billion. Adriatic is unchanged today at A$5.28. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

0016 GMT - Japanese stocks rise as sectors catering to stable domestic demand lead gains. Pharmaceutical and real-estate stocks advance. Chugai Pharmaceutical rises 2.7% and Mitsubishi Estate is 1.2% higher. USD/JPY is at 143.62, compared with 143.10 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Caution over the U.S. economic outlook continues ahead of U.S. jobs data later in the day. In addition to U.S. employment data, investors will be closely watching any developments related to U.S. tariffs. The Nikkei Stock Average rises 0.3% to 37667.17. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

2353 GMT - A visit to Whitehaven Coal's Daunia and Blackwater operations in Australia cements Morgan Stanley's overweight call on its stock. The mines are on track for A$100 million a year in cost savings by the end of FY2025, MS analysts say in a note. They reckon there is also plenty of potential for growth at the sites. However, approvals for the nearby Winchester South development are likely two years away. Morgan Stanley has an A$6.20/share target price on Whitehaven. The stock ended Thursday at A$5.59. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2345 GMT - Japanese stocks may decline as caution over the U.S. economic outlook continues ahead of U.S. jobs data later in the day. Nikkei futures are down 0.3% at 37430 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 143.50, compared with 143.10 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Besides U.S. employment data, investors will be focusing on developments related to U.S. tariffs. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 0.5% to 37554.49 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

2330 GMT - New Zealand media company NZME's latest effort to cut costs can more than offset the effects of tough trading conditions, says Forsyth Barr. NZME is heartened by signs that New Zealand's economy is recovering as interest rates come down. Still, the market isn't improving as much as NZME originally expected. NZME now aims to cut costs by another NZ$8 million. That is on top of NZ$4 million of savings from a restructuring of its newsroom that was announced in January. In response, Forsyth Barr now estimates restructuring costs of some NZ$5 million. "However, these are more than offset by the recently announced cost savings," analyst James Lindsay says. So, Forsyth Barr raises its FY 2025 operating Ebitda forecast by NZ$800,000, to NZ$60.7 million. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2324 GMT - Mangos are a blossoming opportunity for Mission Produce. The fruit producer, whose eggs are mostly in the avocado basket, said Thursday it has become the second-largest mango distributor in the U.S. Mission's mango market share is closing in on 10%, up from 5% a year ago, management said. Mangos are the most-consumed fruit in the world, but their popularity hasn't hit that high in the U.S. Chief Executive Steve Barnard sees that as an opportunity. "What we're seeing in mangos mirrors the early success we achieved with avocados," Barnard said. "Bringing greater consistency and quality to consumers in an underserved market, which drives increased consumption over time." (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

2314 GMT - Morgans pares its earnings view for rural-services provider Elders because it no longer expects the acquisition of Delta Agribusiness will happen in FY 2025. The deal has been held up by competition concerns. The Australian Competition & Consumer Commission wants more time to assess the transaction's effects. Morgans analyst Belinda Moore says there's a risk that the regulator will require Elders to sell some stores to secure approval. Morgans had assumed Delta would contribute A$5 million to Elders's Ebit in FY 2025. That has now been removed. "This has resulted in our FY 2025 Ebit and net profit forecasts falling by 6.6% and 8.8% respectively," Morgans says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2302 GMT - Ord Minnett analyst Milo Ferris is shrugging off Cosol's disappointing FY 2025 earnings guidance. The enterprise asset management software company forecast revenue of A$118 million to A$121 million and Ebitda of A$16.3 million to A$16.7 million. At the midpoint, the guidance was 3.3% and 6.4% below Ord Minnett's forecasts, respectively. Still, Ferris says Cosol's momentum entering the fiscal year appears positive. Revenue growth is expected to be in the high-single-digit percentage range as higher value contracts are locked in. "This appears conservative in our view," Ferris says. The bank retains a buy call on Cosol, which ended Thursday at A$0.655/share. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0254 GMT - PTT Global Chemical faces a prolonged petrochemical downcycle, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Chak Reungsinpinya says in a report. This downcycle may last until 2028 and possibly much longer, owing to massive capacity additions and limited capacity closures, the analyst says. Also, the petrochemical and oil refining company doesn't look very attractive, as its stock trades at 0.68x price-to-tangible book value, the second-most expensive among Thailand downstream players. The brokerage cuts its 2025 earnings estimate for the Thai company by 13% to reflect its lower gross refining margin forecast. It downgrades the stock's rating to sell from hold, with an unchanged target price of THB16.00. Shares last closed at THB20.20. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0238 GMT - Malaysia's stock market is poised for portfolio rebalancing, after FTSE Russell announced that AMMB will replace Hong Leong Financial Group in the benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index following its June semiannual review, say CIMB Securities analysts Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia in a note. The change, effective June 23, could trigger rebalancing activities by passive and index-tracking funds involving the affected constituents, they note. They expect AMMB to carry a higher KLCI weight of 2.7%, compared with Hong Leong's 0.7%. Historical trends suggest that newly added KLCI stocks often outperform. CIMB maintains its end-2025 KLCI target of 1560 and favors CelcomDigi, Public Bank and Tenaga Nasional. The KLCI is 0.1% lower at 1516.27. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0216 GMT - Marco Polo Marine's FY2025 deployment of a new commissioning service operation vessel appears positive, RHB Research's Alfie Yeo says in a report. The vessel has secured charters at robust rates for the next three years and is taking more orders beyond that. The marine logistics group's earnings are likely to be supported by ship chartering from the rest of its fleet, driven by better utilization and charter rates, more vessels, and higher drydock capacity. RHB maintains a buy rating on the stock while trimming the target price to S$0.07 from S$0.08 to reflect reduced FY 2025-2027 earnings forecasts stemming from weaker-than-expected 1H FY 2025 results. Shares are unchanged at S$0.044. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0007 GMT - Oil edges lower in the early morning Asian session amid lingering supply concerns. Prospects of further increases in production by OPEC continue to hang over the market, ANZ Research analysts say in a note. The group approved another output hike of 411,000 barrels per day for July. "We suspect OPEC is taking the opportunity of stronger seasonal demand and a lack of response from non-OPEC producers to regain market share," ANZ adds. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are 0.2% lower at $63.26/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.1% lower at $65.26/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2353 GMT - A visit to Whitehaven Coal's Daunia and Blackwater operations in Australia cements Morgan Stanley's overweight call on its stock. The mines are on track for A$100 million a year in cost savings by the end of FY2025, MS analysts say in a note. They reckon there is also plenty of potential for growth at the sites. However, approvals for the nearby Winchester South development are likely two years away. Morgan Stanley has an A$6.20/share target price on Whitehaven. The stock ended Thursday at A$5.59. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1922 GMT - Oil futures recover the previous day's losses, rising for the third time in four sessions with markets encouraged by President Trump's phone call with China's Xi Jinping to discuss trade and the European Central Bank's interest-rate cut. "Potential U.S. rate cut (September) and the remote possibility a telephone call between global rivals would seal a trade deal supersized the bid today," Mizuho's Robert Yawger says in a note. Trump said he and Xi didn't talk about Russia-Ukraine or Iran, which are ongoing issues that have supported oil prices given U.S. sanctions. WTI settles up 0.8% at $63.37 a barrel, and Brent gains 0.7% to $65.34. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1900 GMT - U.S. natural gas futures end lower in an up-and-down session marked by an above-estimate storage injection and continuing expectations that summer heat will eventually lift demand. The EIA reported a 122 billion-cubic-foot increase in underground storage, raising the surplus over the five-year average to 117 Bcf from 93 Bcf and narrowing the deficit versus last year to 288 Bcf from 316 Bcf. The larger-than-expected build "underscores how record supply continues to outpace early-season cooling demand despite this week's Northeast heat," Gelber & Associates says in a note. Nymex natural gas settles down 1% at $3.677/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1806 GMT - Boxship freight rates jumped to their highest since the start of the year with U.S. importers bringing in more cargo from China on the back of the three-month tariff truce deal between Washington and Beijing. The cost to send a 40-foot container from Shanghai to Los Angeles jumped last week to $5,172 per, up 58% from the week earlier. Transpacific spot rates stood at around $2,000 at the start of the year. "A period of robust trade volumes now looks likely over the summer, particularly on the transpacific," Clarksons said in a research note. (costas.paris@wsj.com)

1636 GMT - Winter wheat shipped by rail costs less than it did last month, the USDA says. In its weekly Grain Transportation Report the rail tariff rate sank for hard red winter wheat sent to ports in Texas. BNSF Railway and Union Pacific both brought their rates down, BNSF by $500 per railcar and UP by $400 per car. The USDA says that the cost to transport winter wheat on these railways is down by as much as 16% from the previous year. CBOT wheat futures are up 0.6% in afternoon trading. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1618 GMT - U.S. natural gas futures turn higher as the market digests a sixth consecutive triple-digit inventory increase, and the largest weekly injection so far this season. "Despite the bearish nature of today's data, the futures market is proving fairly resilient and is on track for weekly gains across the curve," Andy Huenefeld of Pinebrook Energy Advisors says in a note. "Weekly injections should trend lower from here, as cooling load is picking up this week across key population centers." Nymex natural gas is up 0.7% at $3.743/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1612 GMT - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper up 0.95% at $9,740.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.4% at $2,478.0 a ton. Copper climbed as high as $9,809.50 a ton earlier in the session after President Trump said he called China's President Xi Jinping. The tone on his social media post was vague but positive enough to inject some optimism into markets, AJ Bell's Dan Coatsworth says in a note. Avoiding punishing tariffs is crucial if China wants to keep its economic growth plan on track. Anything that relieves pressure on China will read-across positively for the commodities market, given its enormous appetite for metals and minerals, Coatsworth says. Copper miner Antofagasta and iron ore producer Anglo American are among the top risers on the U.K. stock market, he adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1603 GMT - U.S. diesel and gasoline futures pick up from yesterday's selloff that followed larger-than-expected inventory increases and declines in weekly demand. While refineries step up capacity use to meet summer demand for gasoline and jet fuel, demand for diesel typically softens, leading to inventory builds ahead of peak freight and harvest seasons in the fall, says Matt Muenster of transport technology firm Breakthrough. "We continue to see an uneven experience in the truckload market with tonnage generally flat year-over-year thus far into 2025," he says. Nymex diesel is up 1.2% at $2.0948 a gallon. Gasoline is 1.3% higher at $2.0604 a gallon. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Foreign investors buy Indian telecom stocks for sixth straight month in May
data.description:
** Foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) buy telecommunications stocks worth $946 million in May, marking the six consecutive monthly flow into the sector, as per National Securities Depository data

** Telecom stocks (BSESTLIP) jump 3.6% in May, outperform benchmark Sensex , which added 1.5%

** Positive market momentum and strong earnings from sector heavyweight Bharti Airtel among key reasons for jump in sector

** Services sector posted FPI flows worth $933 million in May, second highest flows for a sector

** Overall FPIs bought Indian equities worth $2.32 billion during the month, highest in eight months

** Among other sectors, capital goods and financials recorded inflows during the month, while healthcare and information technology stocks logged outflows

data.title: Zaggle Prepaid Ocean Services to Buy Greenedge for INR270 Million
data.description:
Zaggle Prepaid Ocean Services agreed to acquire Greenedge Enterprises for 270 million Indian rupees.

GreenEdge Enterprises operates as a solution provider for golf travel, experiences, and access-based rewards, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The transaction is expected to be completed in 90 days.

The acquisition will help strengthen Zaggle's product offerings in the loyalty, rewards and travel segments.

data.title: Commonwealth Bank of Australia Adds Benefits, Offers to Customer Recognition Program
data.description:
Commonwealth Bank of Australia said Friday it will add more benefits and offers and boost existing discounts in its customer recognition program, CommBank Yello.

The bank said it will offer up to AU$460 in cashback each year on its products and up to AU$680 in discounts on mobile, national broadband network, and electricity in one year. It will also offer hundreds of dollars each year in cashbacks and discounts on discretionary spending.

The bank lowered the eligibility for the higher tiers of the program to 15 transactions per month from 30, according to a statement. Customers will be able to access a personalized hub of tailored offers in the bank's app.

Eligible business customers will also be able to access benefits to support their business, the bank said.

data.title: US 10-Year Yield Steadies Ahead of May Jobs Report
data.description:
The yield on the US 10-year Treasury note hovered around 4.39% on Friday, stabilizing after a week of heightened volatility as investors awaited the May jobs report for fresh signals on the strength of the US economy.

Treasury yields have faced downward pressure in recent days amid a string of disappointing economic data.

Weak readings on jobless claims, private employment, and an unexpected contraction in services activity have heightened concerns that President Donald Trump’s tariffs may be starting to weigh on growth.

Despite mounting economic uncertainty, Trump has continued to press the Federal Reserve for interest rate cuts.

However, Fed officials have maintained a cautious stance, emphasizing the need for more clarity on the economic outlook and trade developments before adjusting policy.

data.title: Palm rises on strong rival oils, set for fourth weekly gain
data.description:
Malaysian palm oil futures opened higher on Friday, and were set for a fourth consecutive weekly gain, buoyed by stronger rival edible oils, but weaker crude oil prices capped the rise.

The benchmark palm oil contract for August delivery on the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives Exchange added 34 ringgit, or 0.87%, to 3,937 ringgit ($930.73) a metric ton in early trade.

The contract has risen 1.11% so far this week.

FUNDAMENTALS

* Dalian's most-active soyoil contract (DBYcv1) rose 0.89%, while its palm oil contract added 0.79%. Soyoil prices on the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) gained 1.41%.

* Palm oil tracks the price movements of rival edible oils as it competes for a share of the global vegetable oils market.

* Oil prices slipped but were on track for their first weekly gain in three weeks after U.S. President Donald Trump and China's Xi Jinping resumed trade talks, raising hopes for growth and stronger demand in the world's two largest economies. O/R

* Weaker crude oil futures make palm a less attractive option for biodiesel feedstock.

* The ringgit , palm's currency of trade, weakened 0.07% against the dollar, making the commodity slightly cheaper for buyers holding foreign currencies.

* Chicago soybean extended its climb to the fourth straight session and was set for a weekly gain on expectations of improved U.S.-China trade ties, fuelled by the phone call between the leaders of the two countries.

MARKET NEWS

* Asian shares were subdued as investors hunkered down for the all-important U.S. payrolls report, while Tesla suffered huge losses on the very public feud between Trump and billionaire Elon Musk. MKTS/GLOB

DATA/EVENTS

0600 UK Halifax House Prices MM, YY May

0645 France Reserve Assets Total May

0700 EU GDP Revised QQ, YY Q1

1230 UJS Non-Farm Payrolls, Unemployment Rate, Average Earnings YY May

($1 = 4.2300 ringgit)

data.title: Japan Households May Be Drawing Down Savings Amid Inflation — Market Talk
data.description: Japanese government data released earlier today suggests that households started drawing down their savings, as spending hasn't fallen as much as real wages, says Daiwa Securities analyst Eiji Kinouchi. The data showed that household spending decreased 0.1% in April from a year earlier. Inflation-adjusted wages dropped 1.8% during the month, according to separate data released Thursday. "Even if wages increase going forward, it's highly likely that households will continue to replenish their savings for some time," Kinouchi says in a research note. Considering the sluggishness in service prices, the Bank of Japan may hold off on rate hikes for a year or longer, he adds. BOJ Gov. Kazuo Ueda has said he is watching service prices closely. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0246 GMT - Japanese government data released earlier today suggests that households started drawing down their savings, as spending hasn't fallen as much as real wages, says Daiwa Securities analyst Eiji Kinouchi. The data showed that household spending decreased 0.1% in April from a year earlier. Inflation-adjusted wages dropped 1.8% during the month, according to separate data released Thursday. "Even if wages increase going forward, it's highly likely that households will continue to replenish their savings for some time," Kinouchi says in a research note. Considering the sluggishness in service prices, the Bank of Japan may hold off on rate hikes for a year or longer, he adds. BOJ Gov. Kazuo Ueda has said he is watching service prices closely. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0151 GMT - The Philippines central bank could cut rates at its June 19 meeting, Commerzbank Research's analysts say in a research report. The Philippines' May inflation reading was the lowest since November 2019 and stayed below the central bank's target range of 2%-4% for the third straight month, the analysts note. Central bank Gov. Eli Remolona has stated that he expects two rate cuts this year, though these rate reductions might not be consecutive. Commerzbank anticipates two rate cuts in 2H, which would bring the overnight borrowing rate to 5%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0036 GMT - Asian currencies consolidate against the dollar in the morning session. Thursday's phone call between U.S. President Trump and Chinese leader Xi suggests there may be a path to resolving their trade disputes, CBA's Joseph Capurso says. This is positive for the global economy, but "we are sceptical about a quick resolution of their differences," he says. CBA still views the Aug. 12 expiry of the 90-day pause on the U.S.-China trade war as a major downside risk for the Australian dollar against the greenback. AUD/USD edges 0.1% higher to 0.6512; USD/JPY is steady at 143.60. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0016 GMT - Japanese stocks rise as sectors catering to stable domestic demand lead gains. Pharmaceutical and real-estate stocks advance. Chugai Pharmaceutical rises 2.7% and Mitsubishi Estate is 1.2% higher. USD/JPY is at 143.62, compared with 143.10 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Caution over the U.S. economic outlook continues ahead of U.S. jobs data later in the day. In addition to U.S. employment data, investors will be closely watching any developments related to U.S. tariffs. The Nikkei Stock Average rises 0.3% to 37667.17. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0015 GMT - Australia's economy is in a better shape than the tepid growth reported for 1Q suggests, says Aaron Luk, an economist at ANZ. The data revealed strong gains in unit labor costs, which might yet reignite inflation, he says. Within the data, there was plenty of evidence that household incomes are showing robust growth. Both argue against the Reserve Bank of Australia cutting rates in July, he says. It won't take much more on the interest rate front to make households confident enough to spend more. The fall in public demand in 1Q is also unlikely to be the start of a new trend, Luk adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0005 GMT - JGBs edge lower in the morning Tokyo session, tracking overnight price declines in U.S. Treasurys. These market movements come amid risk-on sentiment spurred by President Trump's phone call with Chinese leader Xi on Thursday. Trump called the conversation productive and said both sides agreed to meet soon. However, investors have turned cautious ahead of the key U.S. nonfarm payrolls report due out tonight, Sucden Financial's Research team says in a commentary. The 5-year JGB yield is up 1bp at 1.015%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2345 GMT - Japanese stocks may decline as caution over the U.S. economic outlook continues ahead of U.S. jobs data later in the day. Nikkei futures are down 0.3% at 37430 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 143.50, compared with 143.10 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Besides U.S. employment data, investors will be focusing on developments related to U.S. tariffs. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 0.5% to 37554.49 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

2330 GMT - New Zealand media company NZME's latest effort to cut costs can more than offset the effects of tough trading conditions, says Forsyth Barr. NZME is heartened by signs that New Zealand's economy is recovering as interest rates come down. Still, the market isn't improving as much as NZME originally expected. NZME now aims to cut costs by another NZ$8 million. That is on top of NZ$4 million of savings from a restructuring of its newsroom that was announced in January. In response, Forsyth Barr now estimates restructuring costs of some NZ$5 million. "However, these are more than offset by the recently announced cost savings," analyst James Lindsay says. So, Forsyth Barr raises its FY 2025 operating Ebitda forecast by NZ$800,000, to NZ$60.7 million. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1956 GMT - U.S. Treasury securities declined, sending yields modestly higher, as investors awaited Friday's jobs report to get a stronger handle on the economy's direction in May. The rise in yields came despite an increase in weekly jobless claims to the greatest level since October last week. Expectations are that the report will show a slower pace of hiring last month, with the economy forecast to have added 125,000 new jobs. Trade data from the Commerce Department showed that the U.S. deficit shrank sharply in April, reversing big increases earlier this year as businesses stocked up ahead of tariffs. The benchmark 10-year yield increased to 4.392%, from 4.364% Wednesday. And 2-year yields climbed to 3.924%, from 3.877% Wednesday. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1834 GMT - CIBC says the International Air Transport Association is forecasting 2025 global airline passenger revenue of $693 billion, down from the previous forecast of $705 billion. Analyst Kevin Chiang says this is due to weaker passenger yields and lower traffic due to tempered economic factors. On the cargo front, IATA is forecasting revenue $142 billion versus the previous forecast of $157 billion. Chiang says the revision is due to tariffs that dampened trade volumes and air cargo's lower comparative advantage against ocean cargo as ocean cargo rates decreased. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1549 GMT - Confirmation by the Trump Administration of a phone call between President Trump and Chinese President Xi sends Treasury yields higher. The yield for the 10-year note is now at 4.392%, up 0.033 percentage points. The 2-year note's yield is up 0.035 p.p. to 3.918%. Both turned higher after confirmation that a call took place and trade was discussed by the U.S. and China. The U.S. dollar index trims some of its earlier losses, and is down 0.1% on the day. Pressure may ultimately send the USD index back down, Quasar Elizundia of Pepperstone says in a note. "The US dollar is in a fragile position, extending losses amid an increasingly uncertain economic environment," Elizundia says. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1524 GMT - Canada is likely to show no GDP growth for 2Q as a whole, suggests CIBC Capital Markets' Katherine Judge. It comes after Canadian trade tumbled in April in the wake of President Trump's tariffs. The country's good-trade deficit widened to the largest level on record, with a steep decline in exports to the U.S. The drop in trade and production curtailments, along with the weakening in Canada's labor market, should be enough to see the Bank of Canada cut interest rates by a quarter percentage point in both July and September, Judge says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Ciena (CIEN) Stock Sinks 14% as Incentive Payouts Weigh on Q2 Profit
data.description:
Ciena (CIEN, Financials) shares dropped nearly 14% on Thursday after the networking systems company reported weaker-than-expected fiscal second-quarter earnings, with rising employee compensation costs weighing on profitability.

Ciena reported adjusted earnings per share of $0.42, missing the $0.52 consensus estimate. Revenue rose 24% year over year to $1.13 billion, surpassing the $1.09 billion forecast.

Chief Financial Officer Jim Moylan said on the earnings call that operating expenses climbed to $369 million, entirely due to higher incentive compensation tied to strong order performance. That trend is expected to continue, with Ciena guiding for Q3 operating expenses between $370 million and $375 millionabove the $356 million analyst consensus. Tariffs are also expected to create a $10 million quarterly headwind, though the company believes it can offset most of the impact.

While revenue guidance for the third quarter was positiveprojected between $1.13 billion and $1.21 billion, above the $1.1 billion consensusinvestors were spooked by the elevated expense outlook.

Ciena stock is now deeper in the red for the year, with investors likely watching cost controls and tariff impacts ahead of the next quarterly results.

  • Quick take: See insider trades for CIEN.
  • Explore CIEN chart.

data.title: Singapore Retail Sales Likely to Remain Resilient in 2Q — Market Talk
data.description: Singapore's retail climate should remain resilient in 2Q, RHB Bank's group chief economist and head of market research Barnabas Gan writes in a note. High-profile international events, such as Lady Gaga's concert in May, are expected to boost tourism-related retail activity during the quarter, Gan says. Retail sales could also benefit from government support measures that would aid local retailers, he adds. However, Gan cautions that sales may weaken in 2H amid global headwinds. Singapore's economic outlook remains clouded by global uncertainties, despite a temporary pause in U.S.-China tariff escalations, he says. Households may grow more cautious and reduce discretionary spending, Gan says.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Chatter: Samsung Electronics to Address Trade, Supply Chain Risks in Mid-Year Strategy Talks
data.description:
Samsung Electronics will hold executive strategy meetings from June 17 to June 19 to decide on its business course for the second half of this year, Yonhap News reported Thursday, citing industry sources.

The biannual meetings, led by Device eXperience (DX) division head Roh Tae-moon and Device Solutions (DS) division head Jun Young-hyun, will address global uncertainties, including evolving trade policies under US President Trump's second term, the report said.

The main items on the agenda include region-specific supply chain responses and sales strategies, it said.

Shares of Samsung Electronics rose more than 2% in recent trade on Friday.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: Capital Realm Plans Web3, AI, and Blockchain Investments to Diversify Revenue
data.description:
Capital Realm Financial Holdings said it plans to invest in emerging technologies such as Web3, artificial intelligence, blockchain, robotics, and real-world asset (RWA) tokenization, according to a Thursday Hong Kong bourse filing.

To support the push, the group has opened an account with licensed crypto trading platform OSL Digital Securities, the filing added.

The company expects the investments to diversify revenue streams and potentially generate returns over time. It plans to fund the initiatives using internal resources.

data.title: Vietnam Inflation Rate Rises to 4-Month High
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in Vietnam rose to 3.24% in May 2025, a four-month high, from 3.12% in April.

Price increases accelerated for textiles, footwear, and hats (1.14% vs 1.09% in April), housing and building materials (6.10% vs 5.73%), medicines and medical services (13.58% vs 13.56%), and other goods and services (6.64% vs 6.40%).

In contrast, inflation slowed for food and non-alcoholic beverages (3.72% vs 4.09%), while transport costs continued to fall (-5.66% vs -6.89%).

Meanwhile, core inflation, which excludes volatile items, rose to 3.33% from 3.14%, the highest since October 2023.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices increased by 0.16%, following a 0.07% rise in April.

data.title: Vice President JD Vance Says He Stands by Trump
data.description:
By Natalie Andrews

Vice President JD Vance was uncharacteristically silent for the day, as President Trump and Elon Musk feuded publicly. At about 10:30 p.m. Thursday night he posted on X: "President Trump has done more than any person in my lifetime to earn the trust of the movement he leads," he wrote on X. "I'm proud to stand beside him."

The tweet notably doesn't mention Musk. Vance also taped an episode of Theo Von's podcast, "This Past Weekend," that will air Friday morning.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Calix Says Unit to Scale Back Work on US Projects in Wake of Review of US Government Spending
data.description:
Calix said that its subsidiary business, Leilac, is scaling back work on its projects in the US pending the outcome of ongoing reviews of government spending by the administration of President Donald Trump, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

Leilac's projects were not impacted by the US Department of Energy's recent decision to terminate 24 awards that primarily funded carbon capture and sequestration and decarbonization programs, it noted.

The projects under review include those providing funding for Heirloom's direct air capture project, Project Cypress in Louisiana, as well as for two Leilac carbon capture and storage projects that were selected for awards for engineering studies.

The firm expects that the major project reviews may continue until the end of summer in the US.

Leilac and Calix are ready to increase capacity for project delivery in the US as soon as grant awards are confirmed or other paid customer projects are established, the filing added.

Calix's shares fell 2% in recent trading on Friday.

data.title: Ain Holdings' Profit Drops 19% in Fiscal Year Ended April
data.description:
Ain Holdings profit attributable to owners of the parent dropped 19% to 9.26 billion yen for the fiscal year ended April 30, from 11.4 billion yen.

The pharmacy and retail company's earnings per share (EPS) fell to 264.32 yen from 324.64 yen a year ago, according to a Tokyo bourse filing on June 5.

Net sales jumped 14% to 456.8 billion yen from 399.8 billion yen a year earlier.

It declared a final dividend of 80.00 yen per share for the year, payable from July 31.

For the fiscal year ending April 30, 2026, the company expects attributable profit of 11.45 billion yen, EPS of 326.46 yen, and net sales of 522.0 billion yen.

The company expects to pay a year-end dividend of 80.00 yen per share for the current fiscal year.

data.title: Cordlife Group Appoints Group CEO
data.description:
Cordlife Group appointed Chen Xiaoling as its group chief executive officer, effective June 5, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Friday.

Xiaoling will also serve as the CEO of Singapore, the filing added.

data.title: Cholamandalam Investment accepts bids worth 17.5 billion rupees for bond reissue, bankers say
data.description:
India's Cholamandalam Investment and Finance has accepted bids worth 17.5 billion rupees ($203.9 million) for the reissue of 7.38% May 2027 bonds, three merchant bankers said on Friday.

The company will offer a yield of 7.4271% on this issue and invited bids from bankers and investors on Thursday, they said.

The company did not reply to a Reuters email seeking comment.

data.title: Vietnam Retail Sales Slow in May
data.description:
Vietnam’s retail sales advanced by 10.2% year-on-year in May 2025, easing from an almost two-year high of 11.1% increase in the previous month.

The slowdown was driven by lower sales in tourism (35% vs 46.1%), services (14.3% vs 17%), and goods (8% vs 8.8%).

Meanwhile, trade continued to grow for accommodation and food services (19.1% vs 18.8%).

For the first five months of the year, retail activity expanded by 9.7%.

data.title: Malaysia Stock Market to See Portfolio Rebalancing — Market Talk
data.description: Malaysia's stock market is poised for portfolio rebalancing, after FTSE Russell announced that AMMB will replace Hong Leong Financial Group in the benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index following its June semiannual review, say CIMB Securities analysts Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia in a note. The change, effective June 23, could trigger rebalancing activities by passive and index-tracking funds involving the affected constituents, they note. They expect AMMB to carry a higher KLCI weight of 2.7%, compared with Hong Leong's 0.7%. Historical trends suggest that newly added KLCI stocks often outperform. CIMB maintains its end-2025 KLCI target of 1560 and favors CelcomDigi, Public Bank and Tenaga Nasional. The KLCI is 0.1% lower at 1516.27. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: CapitaLand Ascendas REIT Issues New Units Under Private Placement to Raise SG$500 Million
data.description:
CapitaLand Ascendas REIT issued 202,430,000 new units at SG$2.470 per unit under a private placement, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Friday.

Trading on the new units commenced June 6, the filing said.

The trust raised around SG$500 million in gross proceeds from the placement and has utilized SG$5 million from the proceeds to pay the fees and expenses in relation to the placement.

data.title: Filled Jobs in Australia Fall 0.1% in March Quarter; Marking First Decline Since September 2021 Quarter
data.description:
The number of seasonally adjusted filled jobs in Australia fell 0.1% to 15.9 million in the March quarter, following a 0.3% increase in the prior three-month period, the Australian Bureau of Statistics said Friday.

Total jobs decreased 0.2% to 16.3 million, while job vacancies dropped 4% to 329,600.

"This is the first drop in filled jobs since the September quarter 2021, when lockdowns were introduced to combat the spread of the COVID-19 Delta variant," said Sean Crick, the bureau's head of labor statistics.

"Filled jobs have increased consistently since 2021, marking a total growth of 14.6% over the period," Crick added.

Hours worked rose 0.3% to 5.96 billion hours while average income per employed person lifted 1.4% to AU$24,755.10.

"Hours worked have continued to grow since the December quarter 2023, despite the impact of adverse weather events," Crick added.

On an annual basis, filled jobs rose 2% in the March quarter. Total jobs rose 1.7%, while job vacancies fell 9.4%.
